speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Henry Wermuth, and this is tape number seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Now, ending the last reel, I was marching in a group frantically thinking, I must not go wherever they will lead me because my father isn't here. And I will not go-- I'm not going to be parted with my father. But I knew very well the dangers-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in doing so, yeah. Now, we were marching, and from the assembly place onto the main street which-- it was an old-- by the way, the camp was built on an old Jewish cemetery, and the street was called by the Poles Jerusalemska. I find a bit of mockery in there as well. We were walking on the gravestones. Anyway, this is only by the by. And this street, we reached. And as we reached the street, I could see there were was-- for the first time, I could see-- we turned right, and towards a little house which had some light in front. And in front of that house, there were the SS men and some Jewish police. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And where these people were, the columns turned again left into-- across the street into what I would call a mini camp. This camp was wired anyway. Wires-- wires surround barracks, wires surround the working place, and wires about, you know, everywhere. And there I call it a mini camp, because I hadn't seen it before. And I think there were-- where the Poles actually lived on-- on the sketch map. Only on the sketch-- sort of on a sketch map, they must have been for the later story, which is to come, that the wire in the back must have been extended. But I'll tell that story later, what it has-- that time, it was-- it was something small-- somewhat smaller. And as I say, I call it a mini camp. And I said, well. Whatever it is, I must move through these wires in the mini camp. Once in, it's finished. And I got nearer and nearer and more tense and tense to the lit up spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I drew near, and nearly got into the lit up spot, the lighted place, I heard the commandant say, where is Hilowic? Now, Hilowic is the top of the-- the head of the Jewish police. And I'm not sure what happened, so I'm sort of impulsive that way. I heard the name. I burst out of my-- of my sort of row, of my-- of five. Run away back into the dark assembly place, shouting all the way, Hilowic! Hilowic! Where is Hilowic?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would say that perhaps nobody would stop me when I do this sort of thing. Well, I must have left some confusion in order for them to fill the fives. And if somebody would run after me, I would definitely soon be lost amongst the many others. But nobody did. Then when I reached the groups there, there were the so-called [GERMAN]. Later on, these people were the kapos in the later camps. But here we still called them the [GERMAN]. The [GERMAN] were those who were better positions, still well-dressed, and-- as I say, in commanding positions. They were also called [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Get in line, fives, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where I went there, now I could speak German as well as or better than others. I shouted like that. [SPEAKING GERMAN], in fives, you know. But they looked at me, because I looked like-- not like a [GERMAN]. I just came from work, and I-- you know, but nevertheless they listened to me. Get-- get in line. And-- but I couldn't keep it up for long, you know, in case one of the real ones would ask me. And I tried to get as far back as I could. And so we were marching again. Now what's going to happen this time? I can't do the same trick twice. I mustn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now we reached the street. We turned right. And further we turned left. I must-- and I mustn't get into the light. Although I wasn't afraid that, whatever I would do, that the guard would recognize the boy he had killed about three weeks earlier. Or thought he had. And-- but just as I stepped into the light and I said, oh, too late. Somebody shouted, enough. And a few-- quite a few lines in front of me, they were stopped. And one behind. And they dispersed-- all dispersed back to the-- their own barracks in no time. Within a minute, everything was cleared. Ah, I said. Saved. Back to my father. I'd lost the-- the-- the sense of time. I didn't know would he still be in the metal workshop, or in the barracks. I went past our barracks first. I looked inside. He wasn't there. And I went-- I must confess, slightly proud like a schoolboy with good reports, telling him that not only had I saved myself fourth in line people, but I got out of position where, you know, I was nearly-- that would-- would have nearly been parted from him. And you know, telling proud now what I did. Proudly what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his reaction to all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what was his reaction. You want to know? Tell me what was my reaction when I went to the metal workshops and I found that he had been taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm. No, I can't tell you. Well, imagine what you like. I can't tell you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- at that time the vestige of youth fell away from me. Suddenly I felt grown up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by the nature given to me, I immediately had the-- made a decision. One way or another, either I would try to get him out or I would join him, preferably to get him out. Because who knows what is going to happen to them? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But the nagging thing is I don't know when anything is going to happen. So next morning-- well, I slept alone this time for the first time in my life in the barracks. And in the morning I went to work. And I made sure that I was again selected for the outside work. And there are two like parallel streets in this camp. And the other street we went was behind that camp. Now, on the sketch map, the wires shows nearest the street, the other street. Was-- with my-- when I pass that camp now, my father was much in a different place where the wires were shown there, be halfway only. And the people lined up. Amongst them, my father-- from a distance, I'm not sure. 50 meters, 60. I don't know. And everybody shouted as much as they could. And I passed by, and I also gesticulated like and shouted as I've never shouted before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to come out? Or do I-- should I come in? I don't know if he understood. But of course, whatever he answered, I could not hear. But his gestures was like that. Down. That means I should come there, or maybe he knows something I don't. So at that day, during that day, all I did try-- look at the others. Make-- try to make sure that nobody escaped. So to be twice luckier, I didn't dare to think what-- what would happen if that happened again, if something which I want to do but nobody else should. Anyway, we came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard that the transport would leave in somewhere, transport. Those people from the mini camp will be sent away. But the transport would leave in-- in a day or two or something. I've forgotten it now. I think in-- in my book, I remembered. But the book I've written 10 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, the next morning [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to work. That's the first thing to do. But made sure that I didn't take-- they didn't take me away again. Because you have to show work first. But by-- by doing so, I already had prepared myself. I took-- and we still had bed linen. And I formed like a sack out of duvet covers, or things like that. And I put things in. And I put it on my back. And it wasn't very heavy, but I bent forward as if I was more heavy. And I went to work. And looking out and planning what next to do. My plan was to go to Sygmund, my father's cousin, and he would help me. Well, either me to get in or him to go-- to come out. I didn't know exactly because in this camp, all I knew in the end was where I worked and where I slept. And it's not a great distance, and the rest of the camp I didn't know. Where I worked in-- in the beginning, I didn't know much either. Sort of I-- it's-- and company, I didn't go visiting. I didn't see only-- I think only once I see the-- the-- his wife. During all the 10 months I was in this camp. No-- no social visits or anything. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I walked along the wire towards the kitchen, where I hoped to meet another policeman, which I knew the one, Salz. The one who was hanging that fellow, and the one who gave me food a couple of times. There was a hole in the-- in the wire between the workshop area. I went into the kitchen, back entrance. Nobody there. On the other hand, before I went out, I swiped the kitchen-- a chef's hat. Donned it, put it on. And marched that way with the-- with the white pack, you know, rucksack, so to say, on my back. Towards where I thought was the-- remembered something where the police station was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody stopped me. I went inside. And there somebody on duty asked where Sygmund was, my cousin. He said he wasn't here. Could I wait? You can. I took off my hat, and I was sitting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, the man, said, look. Maybe it's better that you go back to work, because if you're found here, you know what could happen. I said, I can't. But I'll tell you what I want. And I told you that my father is in the what I-- what I was just sort of named the mini camp, and I want to go in or to get somebody to come out. I even thought the little bit of jewelry which we had hidden, if it necessary, I bribe somebody. That would be a good reason for making use of it. And he said, hang on. He went away. And after the short while, somebody came back. And I said, all right. Tomorrow night at 8 o'clock, come here. And you will be exchanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody goes out, and you will go in. And all the time, I was happy, of course. And all the time, I thought that I have helped to be duped. But I was the fool. Then why would somebody get out and me to go in voluntarily?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet it did make no sense because to be killed, he doesn't need to put somebody into another camp. Of course, had I known what I have learned after the war that transports went from there to Auschwitz-- although I didn't know much about Auschwitz yet, but I must have heard the name. Which we come to very soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, next evening I packed my suitcases. Packed everything we had. And I was exchanged for somebody. Went out and I went in. And of course, proudly, you know. My father-- I said to my father, tell me. You asked me to come here. Is there something you know? Said, I know nothing. But what you-- what do you think? You make out as if we are just discussing the venue of the next picnic. Do you think I actually for a minute thought that either you could come in or I could get out? If anything, I thought would be easier than it would be for you to come here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. You know, it sort of-- you know-- still, we were together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this mini camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no name. It was still the-- it didn't-- it was probably the-- the Polish quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think according to the-- what I see of the sketch map, it's probably what they have on the sketch map as the Polish quarters, which I will show at the end. Now-- but the-- but the wires there, as I say, in-- in the sketch map, extended. There were a few mistakes in that sketch map which I have explained in my book. For instance, behind the metal workshop, they put the wires sort-- very near in the sketch, whereas they must have been some distance because between the barracks and-- and the-- and the wire and where the watch-- watchtowers were, there must have been a distance. Because the mountains of-- of--of metal there which we had to select from, and to get to work-- to work with in the-- in the metal workshop. Anyway, next morning, we were sent away. And I would say if ever a hunch paid off, it was my father's. Because what I'm going to tell you now is no less than going from hell into paradise. We were only 50 in a wagon, and we went to a place called Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you chosen, or did everybody go from the mini camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- just always being the last in line, so to say. We came to the last wagons. We went into the last wagons. At a later stage, this being last proved actually no less than fatal. But in this case, it was luck. The last two wagons, the train stopped. The last two wagons were emptied. We were 100 people. And I was-- there wasn't the usual call, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. There was just get out, you know, the normal sort of two wagons. Please come-- you know, not-- I don't know if they said please. But we had to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lined up. And a civilian, very fat and big, with almost a jovial face, called, do you want to have [GERMAN]? [GERMAN] means people with the profession. Well, knowing what [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the work was, that they had always better jobs than just perhaps digging the-- the-- you know, or building streets. I said, oh. Let's go forward. And I pulled my father forward, and we ended up, you know, among about 25 people. He asked everybody, what are you? And my-- because it-- you know, my father said something different to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was going to change. I said, I'm also a painter or something. What my father said. But he had already moved on. And we 25 were chosen in-- as-- to go into a certain camp. The other 75 into a sister camp nearby. I didn't know what he was till he-- till much maybe after the war. I knew it was-- I've heard it was a foundry, and the main work was done in the other camp. For us, it was mainly loading and unloading wagons of stuff, which probably the Germans had looted from somewhere, maybe Ukraine. Now, it was very hard work. But in this camp there were people mostly-- there were-- it was the first camp. And they had all their belongings, and very often money, and other goodies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so they did not eat what was offered, mostly the horse meat. And I-- the-- the people, the Poles who also worked in the camp, were buying other meats and other products on the black market for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had two, three portions as-- as much as I wanted of horse meat. Very hard work and lots to eat. Now, you can imagine I got very, very strong, which probably helped me for the later camps. There's not much to tell. I have been nine months there. During that time, was not a single person killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was at the 20th of July when we heard about Hitler, the-- the main attack on Hitler's life. And it made not a slightest difference to us-- to us whether-- what happening somewhere else. Whatever happened, it happened somewhere else. It didn't happen-- nothing happened. It went on, the daily routine. Nothing happened. In any case, in the end it was to be found out he wasn't killed. Except as we learned later, quite a number of people have been hanged on-- you know, with-- with wire, some of them Hitler's friends, even. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in barracks as you had done previously?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course. Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were they a bit more comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have blankets, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think. Yeah, yeah, yeah. We had a blanket and-- well, straw mattresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the food? Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The food I just mentioned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was plentiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was. In this particular camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody killed. Hard work, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the sanitary arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- very interesting that you ask me that. I have been asked-- I've been interviewed by the Imperial War Museum on 26 reels. And he asked that question. And in the-- I have completely, as I have so many things which I have forgotten. Amongst them the sanitary arrangement in most camps, except the latrines in Plaszow, and later-- which at the end of my story, the toilets in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I can't remember it. But it is nothing special to talk about. We were in barracks, and we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, what else to say? We were not beaten. We had-- we had even-- you know, we had even-- Sundays we were off, unless a wagon had to be unload, and-- and the head of-- sort of the-- the Jewish head of that-- our group, [INAUDIBLE], over 200 there, and-- including 25. And your father was doing the same work as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what he did. Probably as well. Probably as well. But I was glad when Otto Glattstein-- he was from Vienna as well. He was the head of the camp. If he had-- if he was the head of the-- of-- head like the Polish-- the police had just the hat he was wearing. But he was the head of the Jewish police before the ghetto was liquidated. They liquidated all the ghettos one by one. I don't know. But when he-- when we had to work on Sundays, he sometimes took me. But I was glad because he never took my father. And in that camp, there was one old man of approximately, I would say, 60 years old. He was there only because probably he-- he knew somebody. Perfunctory. There were also couples there, two or three with husbands. A great rarity in any camp. The only time I knew about that was in the Jewish police in Plaszow. And nobody else had such arrangements. Where women usually were separated from men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] now I have to switch back. Where was I? You-- you were talking-- well, I had asked you whether your father was working with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked hard, but my father was not taken, not when-- on-- on Sundays. There's not much to be told, maybe that only I heard that this Otto Glattstein had a wife, or was it-- was he the second husband. She had two young sons once. But before they went in the camp, the SS ask him, they can only take one, and what they thought was the father. He had to hold up one of the children to be shot, and throw him on the heap.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I was thinking at the time what would I do. Would I go berserk, throw myself onto the tormentors and be killed, and my wife and probably child with it? Or will I sort of just say by calculation, it's a better chance to go with him. He's dead anyway. Well, that is for everybody to think for themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, in this particular camp nobody was killed. And the 20th, July, I've already spoken about. And shortly after that, a day or two after that, we heard that Russians made the offensive. They were behind Warsaw. They had taken Warsaw. On the way already in Radom, which is halfway between Warsaw and Kielce where I-- where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this, were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 20, July, '44, was the famous assassination attempt on Hitler. Mine didn't count because he wasn't killed, and I was-- I-- sort of a-- nobody sort of looked after me. Either they sort of-- they kept quiet about it not to give succor to the party's enemies, to the Nazis' enemies. I'm not sure. Long story short, we are now at-- into the 20th day of July. And we heard the Russians had made an offensive. And there was rumors that we would be evacuated. The further rumors that the-- if you would be evacuated, the-- the administration would try to find us a good place. Maybe it's wishful thinking. But that what it was. A number of boys which we-- which we didn't know who, but there was again rumors within the camp, would escape to the partisans. And I said to my father, let's join. Let's join. But everybody sort of-- nobody admitted to being the ones who would go. And I-- later I was told probably they would think my father was too old for that. Little did they know that he was-- just by-- by one example, in this particular camp there was even once a football match arranged. And there, my father came on his own. And I was rather proud because he showed the youngsters how to play, and how, and he was 48. And well, the day came, the 31st of July, a recreation day. And we went outside. And outside at the station, at the train, we met with the others, of the other sister camp. They-- we were 200. The others were 400. 600 mostly young, strong people. Now, if anybody knows about the history of these camps, the old, the weak, and the children were eliminated as soon as they reached camp, usually. In-- especially in Auschwitz and the other camps of that type. The young had a better chance. Young, strong. Slave work. They needed slave-- slaves for the-- furthering the war effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- So you were rounded up to-- to leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not rounded up.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just asked to go to the train, and we went. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it far?

speaker: interviewee
text: A number of people-- a number of people-- I'm not sure. 2, 4, 6, or 8 had escaped. All that it is between the two factories, there were sort of like a traffic between horse-drawn carts being manufactured and being pushed from one way to another, and what we had to do I'm not quite sure. But a group has taken such a number of carts. And while they were pushing out of our gate towards the other, the-- the guards wouldn't even watch because that's almost a daily thing to happen. Only they did not go into the other camp. They disappeared in the woods.
